petitioner_background: The petitioner sought classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences. The decision does not specify the petitioner's nationality, specific field within the sciences, or detailed career qualifications. The Texas Service Center director denied the petition, finding the petitioner had not established the requisite extraordinary ability through extensive documentation and sustained national or international acclaim.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO summarily dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to specifically identify any erroneous conclusion of law or statement of fact, as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v). Counsel raised the argument for consideration of 'comparable evidence' under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4) for the first time on appeal, but the AAO held that comparable evidence is only permitted when the ten criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3) do not readily apply to the beneficiary's occupation — not simply because an alien is unable to meet three of the regulatory criteria. Counsel did not specifically challenge any of the director's findings or analyses of evidence submitted for the regulatory criteria, and the appellate submission included no arguments or evidence addressing the criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Counsel also indicated no supplemental brief or additional evidence would be submitted.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple procedural issues: (1) The petitioner's original appeal was rejected by the AAO as untimely and returned to the director for treatment as a motion to reconsider; (2) The director then treated the late appeal as a motion to reopen and reconsider, dismissed the motion, and affirmed denial; (3) On the current appeal, counsel raised the 'comparable evidence' argument under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4) for the first time, having not raised it in the initial submission, RFE response, or motion; (4) Counsel failed to specifically identify any erroneous conclusion of law or statement of fact, triggering summary dismissal under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v); (5) Counsel indicated no supplemental brief or additional evidence would be submitted.
